chapters.name: Access Control
chapters.desc: Auth, RBAC, MAC, DAC, Bell-LaPadula, Biba

questions.question: In the context of software security, what is a 'policy'?
options: A concrete implementation that enforces rules
A specification of good (secure) or bad (insecure) software behavior
A programming language feature for access control
A hardware component that monitors system calls
questions.explanation: A policy defines the 'law' — it specifies what behaviors are considered secure or insecure. The mechanism is the concrete enforcer.

questions.question: What is the relationship between a mechanism and a policy?
questions.explanation: A mechanism is the concrete implementation that ensures software adheres to (enforces) a policy.

questions.question: What are the two-word summaries of software security given in the course?
questions.explanation: The two key two-word summaries are 'Minimize trust' and 'Validate inputs' — foundational principles of secure coding.

questions.question: According to the course, what is described as 'the biggest problem in security in computer science'?
options: The gap between high-level and low-level policies
The lack of secure programming languages
questions.explanation: The gap between high-level policies (what we want) and low-level policies (what we can implement) is identified as the biggest problem in security.

questions.question: What is a trace in the context of program security?
questions.explanation: A trace is a sequence of security-relevant actions delimited by semicolons. A program is the set of all possible traces it may exhibit.

questions.question: What does it mean for a program to be 'diverging'?
options: It is non-terminating (produces an infinite trace)
questions.explanation: A diverging program is non-terminating — it produces an infinite trace. A converging program terminates (finite trace).

questions.question: The empty trace (ε) represents a program doing nothing at all.
questions.explanation: The empty trace represents a program doing no security-relevant actions. The program may still be doing work — just nothing that is security-relevant.

questions.question: A trace is a prefix of itself.
questions.explanation: By definition, a ≤ a — every trace is a prefix of itself. Also, the empty trace ε is a prefix of all traces.

questions.question: The empty trace is denoted by the symbol λ (lambda).
questions.explanation: The empty trace is denoted by ε (epsilon), NOT λ (lambda). This is a common misconception noted in the course.

questions.question: A program P can be formally defined as:
options: The set of all possible traces it may exhibit
A mapping from inputs to outputs
questions.explanation: A program is defined as the set of all possible traces it may exhibit. This set-based definition is key to formal security reasoning.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a policy and a mechanism, and give one example of each.
questions.answer: A policy is a specification of secure/insecure behavior (e.g., 'no unauthorized access to files'). A mechanism is a concrete enforcer of a policy (e.g., file permissions, firewalls, or access control lists).

questions.question: What does the empty trace ε represent, and why is this distinction important?
questions.answer: The empty trace ε represents a program performing no security-relevant actions — not that it does nothing. This matters because a program might be busy computing but if none of its actions are security-relevant, its trace is empty.

questions.question: A company's security team writes a document stating 'All customer data must be encrypted at rest and in transit.' An engineer then implements TLS for network traffic and AES-256 for database storage. In formal terms, what did each party produce?
options: The team wrote a mechanism; the engineer created a policy
The team wrote a policy; the engineer created mechanisms
Both created policies at different abstraction levels
Both created mechanisms targeting different threat vectors
questions.explanation: The security document is the policy (specification of desired behavior). TLS and AES-256 implementations are mechanisms (concrete enforcers of that policy).

questions.question: Consider a program that reads a file, processes its contents in memory, and writes output. If the security policy only concerns file access, which parts of the execution appear in the trace?
options: Only the file read and file write operations
The entire execution trace including CPU cycles
questions.explanation: Traces only contain security-relevant actions. If the policy only concerns file access, only the file read and write are security-relevant — memory processing is invisible to the trace.

questions.question: A single mechanism can only enforce one policy.
questions.explanation: There is a many-to-many relationship between mechanisms and policies. A single mechanism can enforce multiple policies, and a single policy can be enforced by multiple mechanisms.

questions.question: If trace t₁ = 'read; write' and trace t₂ = 'read; write; delete', which statement is true?
options: t₂ is a prefix of t₁
t₁ is a prefix of t₂
Neither is a prefix of the other
questions.explanation: t₁ ≤ t₂ because t₁ (read; write) is a prefix of t₂ (read; write; delete). A prefix is any initial subsequence of a trace.

questions.question: An online store has a policy: 'Never charge a customer twice for the same order.' The payment system processes 1000 transactions per second. Is this policy best enforced by a static or dynamic mechanism, and why?
options: Static — analyze the source code for duplicate charge bugs
Dynamic — a runtime monitor can check each transaction against previous ones in real-time
Neither — this policy cannot be enforced
questions.explanation: This requires runtime monitoring of actual transaction data — you can't determine at compile time whether duplicate charges will occur. A dynamic mechanism checking each transaction is appropriate.

questions.question: A program has traces: {read; write; close}, {read; close}, {read; write; write; close}. How many traces does this program have?
questions.explanation: The program is defined as the SET of all its possible traces. This set contains exactly 3 distinct traces.

questions.question: When mechanism M ensures software obeys policy P, we say M _____ P.
questions.explanation: The formal terminology is that mechanism M 'enforces' policy P — it ensures the software adheres to the policy.

questions.question: A converging program produces an infinite trace.
questions.explanation: A converging program is terminating and produces a finite trace. A diverging program is non-terminating and produces an infinite trace.

questions.question: What does the 'C' in the CIA triad stand for?
questions.explanation: The CIA triad stands for Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability — the three core objectives of information security.

questions.question: A property is a policy that can be evaluated by examining:
options: The entire set of all traces at once
questions.explanation: A property is a special kind of policy where each trace can be evaluated independently — you don't need to look at other traces to decide if a single trace violates the policy.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a safety property?
options: Nothing bad ever happens — bad traces are NOT remediable
questions.explanation: Safety properties state 'nothing bad happens.' If a trace violates a safety property, it cannot be remedied — no future actions can undo the violation.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a liveness property?
options: Good things eventually happen — bad traces ARE remediable
questions.explanation: Liveness properties state 'something good eventually happens.' If a trace currently violates liveness, it can potentially be remedied by extending the trace with future actions.

questions.question: 'The system must eventually respond to every request.' Is this a safety or liveness property?
options: Liveness — the 'bad' trace (no response yet) can always be remedied by eventually responding
questions.explanation: This is a liveness property. If the system hasn't responded yet, the trace can be extended with a response to fix it. The bad prefix is remediable.

questions.question: 'The system must never disclose a user's password in plaintext.' Is this a safety or liveness property?
options: Liveness — the system can undo disclosure later
Safety — once the password is disclosed, the damage cannot be undone
questions.explanation: This is a safety property. Once a password is disclosed in plaintext, no future action can undo that disclosure. The bad trace is not remediable.

questions.question: All policies are properties.
questions.explanation: Not all policies are properties. A property evaluates traces independently, but some policies require comparing traces to each other (e.g., information flow policies like noninterference).

questions.question: Confidentiality means ensuring data has not been tampered with.
questions.explanation: That describes integrity, not confidentiality. Confidentiality means ensuring data is only accessible to authorized parties.

questions.question: Availability means ensuring authorized users can access resources when needed.
questions.explanation: Availability is the guarantee that systems and data are accessible to authorized users when needed. DoS attacks target availability.

questions.question: A hospital database is modified by an unauthorized user, changing a patient's blood type from O+ to A-. Which CIA principle was primarily violated?
questions.explanation: Integrity was violated — the data was tampered with by an unauthorized party. This is especially dangerous in healthcare where incorrect data can be life-threatening.

questions.question: A DDoS attack takes down an online banking portal for 6 hours. Which CIA principle was primarily violated?
questions.explanation: Availability was violated — authorized users could not access the service. DDoS attacks specifically target availability.

questions.question: An attacker intercepts and reads encrypted emails by obtaining the decryption key. Which CIA principle was violated?
options: None — encrypted data is always safe
questions.explanation: Confidentiality was violated — an unauthorized party gained access to read private communications.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a safety property and a liveness property. Give one example of each.
questions.answer: A safety property says 'nothing bad happens' — once violated, the trace cannot be remedied (e.g., 'the system never leaks passwords'). A liveness property says 'something good eventually happens' — a currently-bad trace can be fixed by future actions (e.g., 'every request eventually gets a response').

questions.question: Why is the distinction between 'properties' and 'non-property policies' important for mechanism design?
questions.answer: Properties evaluate individual traces independently, making them easier to enforce with runtime monitors. Non-property policies (like noninterference) require comparing multiple traces, which is much harder to enforce dynamically since a monitor only sees one execution at a time.

questions.question: Consider a policy P that says: 'A program's output must be the same regardless of whether the secret input is 0 or 1.' This requires comparing two execution traces. Is P a property?
options: Yes — it evaluates each trace independently
No — it requires comparing traces from different executions, making it a non-property policy
Yes — it's a safety property about outputs
No — but only because it involves secret inputs
questions.explanation: This is noninterference, a classic non-property policy. You must compare traces from two different executions (with different secret inputs) to check it, so individual traces cannot be evaluated independently.

questions.question: Every security policy can be expressed as a property.
questions.explanation: Not every policy is a property. Information flow policies like noninterference require comparing traces and cannot be decomposed into per-trace checks.

questions.question: Which CIA principle does encryption primarily protect?
questions.explanation: Encryption primarily protects confidentiality by making data unreadable to unauthorized parties. (It can also support integrity via authenticated encryption, but its primary purpose is confidentiality.)

questions.question: A policy states: 'If a file is ever marked as deleted, it must eventually be removed from disk.' A program marks a file as deleted but hasn't removed it yet. Can this trace still satisfy the policy?
options: No — the deletion mark without removal is a permanent violation
Yes — this is a liveness property, so the trace can be extended to include the actual removal
It depends on the file size
No — marking as deleted automatically violates integrity
questions.explanation: This is a liveness property — the 'bad' state (marked but not yet removed) is remediable. Extending the trace to include the removal action satisfies the policy.

questions.question: A policy states: 'The total number of failed login attempts across ALL users must never exceed 10,000 in one hour.' Is this a property?
options: Yes — you can check it trace by trace
No — it requires aggregating information across multiple users' traces
Yes — it's a simple counter check
questions.explanation: This requires correlating information across multiple traces (different users' login attempts), so it cannot be evaluated by examining individual traces independently. It's therefore not a property in the formal sense.

questions.question: Which CIA property does a digital signature primarily protect?
questions.explanation: Digital signatures primarily protect integrity — they allow you to verify that data hasn't been tampered with. They also provide authentication and non-repudiation, but their primary CIA contribution is integrity.

questions.question: A policy states: 'The system must never execute unauthorized code.' Is this a safety or liveness property?
options: Liveness — the system can stop executing unauthorized code later
Safety — once unauthorized code is executed, the violation cannot be undone
questions.explanation: This is a safety property. Once unauthorized code has been executed, no future action can undo that execution. The bad event is irremediable.

questions.question: Consider two policies: P₁ = 'No file is ever deleted' (safety) and P₂ = 'Every deleted file is eventually recovered' (liveness). If both are enforced simultaneously, what happens when a file is deleted?
options: P₂ kicks in and recovers the file
The deletion violates P₁ irremediably — P₁ (safety) is already broken regardless of P₂
P₁ prevents the deletion from happening in the first place if properly enforced
Both policies are satisfied as long as recovery happens
questions.explanation: If P₁ is properly enforced (preventatively), the deletion never occurs in the first place. The mechanism for P₁ must prevent deletions, making P₂ moot. If P₁ is only monitored (not prevented), then deletion breaks P₁ irremediably.

questions.question: Integrity ensures that data is accessible to authorized users when needed.
questions.explanation: That describes availability. Integrity ensures that data has not been tampered with or modified by unauthorized parties.

questions.question: What is a static mechanism?
options: A mechanism that runs at runtime
A white-box analysis mechanism that examines source code without executing it
A mechanism that monitors network traffic
questions.explanation: A static mechanism performs white-box analysis — it examines the source code or program structure without executing it. Type checkers are a classic example.

questions.question: What is a dynamic mechanism?
options: A mechanism that analyzes source code
A mechanism that compiles code with extra checks
A black-box mechanism that monitors program behavior at runtime
A mechanism that encrypts data in motion
questions.explanation: A dynamic mechanism is a black-box runtime monitor — it observes the program's behavior during execution. Firewalls and intrusion detection systems are examples.

questions.question: A sound enforcer guarantees:
options: No false positives (never rejects safe programs)
No false negatives (never accepts violating programs)
No false positives AND no false negatives
questions.explanation: A sound enforcer has no false negatives — it never misses a violation. It may have false positives (rejecting safe programs), but it will catch all bad programs.

questions.question: A complete enforcer guarantees:
options: No false positives (never rejects safe programs)
questions.explanation: A complete enforcer has no false positives — it never incorrectly rejects a safe program. It may have false negatives (missing some violations).

questions.question: A precise enforcer would need to be:
options: Both sound and complete — no false positives AND no false negatives
questions.explanation: A precise enforcer is both sound (no false negatives) and complete (no false positives). In general, achieving a precise enforcer is impossible due to the Halting Problem.

questions.question: Why can't a perfect static mechanism exist for all properties?
options: The Halting Problem proves it's impossible to determine all program behaviors statically
Static mechanisms can't read source code
questions.explanation: The Halting Problem proves that no algorithm can determine whether an arbitrary program halts. Since a perfect static mechanism would need to determine all possible behaviors, it's generally impossible.

questions.question: A type checker is an example of a static mechanism.
questions.explanation: Type checkers analyze source code at compile time (without executing it) to enforce type safety — a classic static, white-box mechanism.

questions.question: A firewall is an example of a static mechanism.
questions.explanation: A firewall is a dynamic mechanism — it monitors and filters network traffic at runtime (black-box, runtime monitoring).

questions.question: It is generally possible to build a precise enforcer for any security property.
questions.explanation: A precise enforcer (both sound and complete) is generally impossible due to undecidability results like the Halting Problem.

questions.question: Your company's code scanner flags 200 functions as 'potentially vulnerable to SQL injection.' After manual review, only 30 actually have vulnerabilities. The scanner is:
options: Sound but not complete — it catches all issues but has false positives
Complete but not sound — it misses no safe code but has false negatives
Precise — it found the exact set of vulnerabilities
Neither sound nor complete — it's just unreliable
questions.explanation: The scanner is sound (it found all 30 real vulnerabilities — no false negatives) but not complete (170 false positives — safe functions flagged as vulnerable).

questions.question: An antivirus program is known to never flag legitimate software as malware, but occasionally misses actual malware. This antivirus is:
questions.explanation: It's complete (no false positives — never flags clean software) but not sound (has false negatives — misses some real malware).

questions.question: What is a False Positive (Type I error) in the context of a security mechanism?
options: Incorrectly flagging a secure program as insecure
questions.explanation: A False Positive (Type I error) means the mechanism incorrectly rejects or flags a secure program. It's a 'false alarm.'

questions.question: What is a False Negative (Type II error) in the context of a security mechanism?
options: Incorrectly flagging a secure program as insecure
Accepting an insecure program as if it were secure
questions.explanation: A False Negative (Type II error) means the mechanism fails to catch a real violation — it incorrectly accepts an insecure program.

questions.question: Explain why dynamic mechanisms are good for enforcing properties but bad for non-properties.
questions.answer: Dynamic mechanisms observe a single execution trace at runtime. Properties can be evaluated on individual traces, so a runtime monitor can check them. Non-properties (like noninterference) require comparing multiple traces from different executions, which a runtime monitor cannot do since it only sees one execution at a time.

questions.question: A security team must choose between a sound scanner and a complete scanner for a critical medical device. Which should they prefer and why?
questions.answer: For a critical medical device, they should prefer the sound scanner (no false negatives). Missing a real vulnerability (false negative) in a medical device could endanger lives. False positives (flagging safe code) are annoying but safe — they just mean more manual review. In safety-critical systems, it's better to over-flag than to miss real issues.

questions.question: Which of the following is a True Positive?
options: An insecure program is correctly flagged as insecure
questions.explanation: A True Positive is when the mechanism correctly identifies and flags an insecure program — the 'positive' detection is true/correct.

questions.question: Dynamic mechanisms can perfectly enforce non-property policies like noninterference.
questions.explanation: Dynamic mechanisms only see one execution trace at a time. Non-property policies like noninterference require comparing multiple traces, making them impossible to perfectly enforce dynamically.

questions.question: You need to enforce a property P on critical infrastructure. You have two tools: Tool A is sound but not complete, and Tool B is complete but not sound. What is the best strategy?
options: Use both tools together — run A first to catch all violations, then B to reduce false positives
Use neither — build a precise tool instead
questions.explanation: Using both tools together gives you the best of both worlds. Tool A (sound) ensures no real violations are missed. Tool B (complete) can help verify that flagged items are real issues, reducing false positives from Tool A.

questions.question: A compiler's type checker rejects a program that would actually be safe at runtime. This is an example of:
options: False Positive (Type I error) — the mechanism is being sound at the cost of completeness
questions.explanation: The type checker incorrectly rejects a safe program — a false positive. Type checkers are typically sound (catch all type errors) but incomplete (may reject some actually-safe programs), especially for dynamic features.

questions.question: If a mechanism has BOTH false positives and false negatives, it is:
questions.explanation: Sound means no false negatives. Complete means no false positives. Having both types of errors means the mechanism is neither sound nor complete.

questions.question: An intrusion detection system (IDS) is an example of a:
questions.explanation: An IDS is a dynamic mechanism — it monitors system/network behavior at runtime (black-box analysis) to detect potential security violations.

questions.question: Explain the Halting Problem and its relevance to static analysis mechanisms in security.
questions.answer: The Halting Problem (proved by Turing) states that no general algorithm can determine whether an arbitrary program will halt or run forever. This means no static mechanism can perfectly analyze all program behaviors — it's fundamentally impossible to build a precise static analyzer for all properties. This is why type checkers and static analyzers must be either sound (catching all issues but with false positives) or complete (no false positives but missing some issues), never both.

questions.question: A static mechanism can catch bugs at runtime.
questions.explanation: Static mechanisms analyze code without executing it (at compile/analysis time), not at runtime. Dynamic mechanisms operate at runtime.

questions.question: A firewall that blocks unauthorized network traffic before it reaches the server is an example of which mechanism category?
questions.explanation: A firewall is a preventative mechanism — it acts before an attack reaches its target, blocking unauthorized traffic proactively.

questions.question: Audit logs and intrusion detection systems are examples of which mechanism category?
questions.explanation: Audit logs and IDS are detective mechanisms — they identify attacks after they have occurred or while they are in progress.

questions.question: Restoring a system from a known-good backup after a breach is an example of:
questions.explanation: Restoring from backup is a recovery mechanism — it reverts the system to a known-good state after an attack.

questions.question: Running critical services on multiple replicated servers so that compromising one doesn't take down the system is an example of:
questions.explanation: Replication for damage isolation is a containment mechanism — it prevents a compromise of one component from affecting the entire system.

questions.question: What is polymorphic malware?
options: Malware that only targets specific operating systems
Malware that changes its code signature with each infection to evade detection
Malware that spreads through USB drives only
Malware written in multiple programming languages
questions.explanation: Polymorphic malware mutates its code with each infection, changing its signature. This makes it very difficult for signature-based detective mechanisms (like antivirus) to detect.

questions.question: Why does polymorphic malware specifically challenge detective mechanisms?
options: It runs too fast for monitors to catch
Its changing signature evades signature-based detection
questions.explanation: Detective mechanisms like antivirus often rely on known malware signatures. Polymorphic malware changes its signature with each infection, making signature matching fail.

questions.question: A company discovers a ransomware attack that has also exfiltrated sensitive customer data. Even if they restore from backups, why is the situation still critical?
options: Backups are always corrupted by ransomware
The ransomware will just re-infect the system
Recovery only restores availability and integrity — the exfiltrated data means confidentiality is permanently compromised
They need to pay the ransom regardless
questions.explanation: Modern ransomware often includes data exfiltration. While backups restore availability and integrity (recovery mechanism), the stolen data means confidentiality has been irrevocably breached — you can't 'un-steal' data.

questions.question: Preventative mechanisms act before an attack occurs.
questions.explanation: Preventative mechanisms are proactive — they attempt to prevent attacks before they happen (e.g., firewalls, passwords, input validation).

questions.question: Detective mechanisms can prevent attacks from occurring.
questions.explanation: Detective mechanisms identify attacks after they occur or while in progress — they cannot prevent them. You need preventative mechanisms for prevention.

questions.question: Containment mechanisms work by isolating damage through replication.
questions.explanation: Containment mechanisms use replication and isolation to ensure that if one component is compromised, the damage doesn't spread to the entire system.

questions.question: A system uses: (1) a WAF to block SQL injection, (2) database query logging, (3) nightly backups, and (4) microservice isolation. Match each to its mechanism category in order.
questions.explanation: WAF = preventative (blocks before reaching DB), query logging = detective (detects after the fact), backups = recovery (restore after breach), microservice isolation = containment (limits blast radius).

questions.question: Explain why a comprehensive security strategy needs all four mechanism categories (preventative, detective, recovery, containment) rather than just one.
questions.answer: No single category is sufficient: preventative mechanisms can be bypassed (zero-days), detective mechanisms only find attacks after damage begins, recovery mechanisms can't undo confidentiality breaches, and containment limits but doesn't prevent damage. A layered approach ensures that if one category fails, others provide backup. This is defense in depth.

questions.question: Which mechanism category is LEAST effective against data exfiltration once it has occurred?
questions.explanation: Recovery (e.g., restoring backups) cannot undo data exfiltration — once data is stolen, you can't 'un-steal' it. Prevention and containment are better at addressing exfiltration.

questions.question: A new strain of polymorphic ransomware is detected. For each mechanism category, describe one specific countermeasure you would deploy.
questions.answer: Preventative: deploy email filtering and input validation to block initial infection vectors. Detective: use behavior-based (not signature-based) anomaly detection to identify ransomware activity. Recovery: maintain offline, immutable backups to restore encrypted systems. Containment: use network segmentation and least-privilege access to limit lateral movement.

questions.question: An organization experiences a zero-day exploit that bypasses their firewall. Their IDS detects unusual traffic patterns 10 minutes later, and automated systems isolate the affected servers. Which mechanism categories were involved, in order?
questions.explanation: The firewall (preventative) failed against the zero-day. The IDS (detective) identified the attack. The automated isolation (containment) limited the damage. This illustrates why defense in depth with multiple categories is essential.

questions.question: Which mechanism category is an antivirus scanner that runs weekly scans?
questions.explanation: A weekly antivirus scan is a detective mechanism — it identifies malware that has already infected the system. Real-time scanning can be more preventative, but scheduled scans are primarily detective.

questions.question: A password requirement before accessing a system is an example of which mechanism category?
questions.explanation: Passwords are a preventative mechanism — they prevent unauthorized access before any malicious action can occur.

questions.question: A containment mechanism attempts to prevent attacks before they happen.
questions.explanation: Containment mechanisms isolate damage after or during a compromise via replication and segmentation. Preventative mechanisms are the ones that act before attacks.

questions.question: Which secure design principle states that every input should be checked for validity before processing?
questions.explanation: 'Sanitize All Inputs' means treating every input as potentially malicious and validating/cleaning it before use, reducing the attack surface.

questions.question: Which principle states that a user or process should only have the minimum permissions necessary to complete its task?
questions.explanation: The Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP) states that every subject should operate with the minimum set of privileges needed — no more.

questions.question: 'Defense in Depth' recommends using multiple layers of security. Why should these layers be heterogeneous (different types)?
options: If all layers use the same mechanism, a single exploit can bypass all of them
Different types look more professional to auditors
questions.explanation: Heterogeneous layers ensure that an exploit for one layer doesn't automatically bypass all layers. If all layers are the same, one vulnerability compromises everything.

questions.question: Why is 'security by obscurity' considered a bad practice?
options: Keeping mechanisms secret provides no real security — attackers can reverse-engineer or discover the hidden details
questions.explanation: Security by obscurity relies on attackers not knowing how the system works. Once the secret is discovered (through reverse engineering, leaks, etc.), all security is lost. Real security should withstand full knowledge of the mechanism.

questions.question: Which principle recommends keeping security designs as simple as possible?
questions.explanation: The Simplicity principle states that security mechanisms should be as simple as possible — in design, code, and user interaction. Complex systems have more potential for bugs and misconfigurations.

questions.question: According to the 'Secure the Weakest Link' principle, what is often the weakest link in a security system?
questions.explanation: Users are often the weakest link. Social engineering, phishing, and poor password practices bypass technical controls entirely. Attackers target the easiest entry point.

questions.question: How should errors be handled according to secure design principles?
options: Display detailed error messages to help users debug
Fail silently with no indication of the error
Handle errors securely — don't leak information, and fail to secure defaults
Log errors only in production, ignore them in development
questions.explanation: Errors should be handled securely: don't leak sensitive information (stack traces, database details), and when a failure occurs, default to a secure state rather than an open/permissive one.

questions.question: A web application shows the error: 'ERROR: PostgreSQL 14.2 on port 5432 — column "password" does not exist in table "users_legacy"'. Which TWO secure design principles are violated?
options: Handle Errors Securely and Avoid Security by Obscurity
Sanitize Inputs and Defense in Depth
Secure the Weakest Link and Key Storage
questions.explanation: The error leaks sensitive internal details (database type, version, port, table structure) — violating 'Handle Errors Securely.' Exposing internal architecture also violates 'Avoid Security by Obscurity' by giving attackers reconnaissance data.

questions.question: A developer stores API keys directly in source code committed to a public GitHub repository. Which principle(s) are violated?
options: Key Storage and Avoid Security by Obscurity
questions.explanation: Storing keys in public source code violates Key Storage (keys should be in secure locations like TPMs or environment variables) and effectively relies on obscurity (hoping nobody looks at the repo).

questions.question: Defense in Depth means using a single, very strong security mechanism.
questions.explanation: Defense in Depth means using MULTIPLE LAYERS of different (heterogeneous) security mechanisms, not relying on a single strong one. If one layer fails, others still provide protection.

questions.question: The Principle of Least Privilege applies only to human users, not to software processes.
questions.explanation: PoLP applies to all subjects — human users, software processes, services, API tokens, etc. Every entity should operate with the minimum necessary permissions.

questions.question: A startup builds a login system where the algorithm for hashing passwords is kept secret as the primary security measure. Critique this approach using at least two secure design principles.
questions.answer: This violates 'Avoid Security by Obscurity' — the algorithm could be discovered via reverse engineering or leaks, leaving passwords unprotected. It also violates 'Simplicity' by creating a custom secret algorithm instead of using well-tested standards like bcrypt or Argon2. They should use proven, open algorithms where security doesn't depend on keeping the method secret.

questions.question: List all 8 secure design principles covered in this course.
questions.answer: 1. Sanitize All Inputs, 2. Handle Errors Securely, 3. Defense in Depth, 4. Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP), 5. Avoid Security by Obscurity, 6. Key Storage (use TPM, secure locations), 7. Simplicity, 8. Secure the Weakest Link.

questions.question: Where should cryptographic keys ideally be stored according to the Key Storage principle?
options: In a Trusted Platform Module (TPM) or hardware security module
questions.explanation: Keys should be stored in secure locations like TPMs or HSMs, with minimal exposure time. Never in source code, config files, or other easily accessible locations.

questions.question: A developer adds input validation on the client-side JavaScript form. A security reviewer says this is insufficient. Which principle explains why, and what should be done?
options: Key Storage — the validation keys need to be server-side
Sanitize All Inputs — client-side validation can be bypassed; server-side validation is essential
Defense in Depth — add encryption to the form
Simplicity — remove the client-side validation entirely
questions.explanation: Client-side validation can be trivially bypassed by modifying requests directly. 'Sanitize All Inputs' requires server-side validation. Client-side checks are nice for UX but are NOT a security control.

questions.question: An application runs its web server process as root. Which principle is violated?
questions.explanation: Running a web server as root gives it far more permissions than needed. PoLP dictates it should run with the minimum privileges required to serve web pages.

questions.question: The Key Storage principle recommends minimizing the duration that keys exist in memory.
questions.explanation: Keys should be stored securely and for the minimum necessary duration. The longer a key exists in accessible memory, the greater the window for extraction.

questions.question: A bank's mobile app requires a 32-character password with special characters, changed every 2 weeks, and disallows password managers. Users write passwords on sticky notes. Which principle explains why this approach fails?
options: Defense in Depth — they need more layers
Simplicity and Secure the Weakest Link — overly complex security drives users to insecure workarounds
Sanitize Inputs — passwords should be validated
Key Storage — sticky notes aren't TPMs
questions.explanation: Overly complex password requirements violate Simplicity (user interaction should be simple) and create the very weakness that 'Secure the Weakest Link' warns about — users resort to insecure workarounds like sticky notes.

questions.question: A system defaults to granting full access when its authentication server is unreachable. Which principle is violated?
options: Handle Errors Securely — failing should default to the most restrictive (secure) state
questions.explanation: When errors occur, the system should fail to a secure default state (deny access). Defaulting to full access when auth fails is a classic 'fail open' vulnerability.

questions.question: A web application has 3 identical firewalls in series. A security auditor criticizes this design. Why?
options: Three firewalls cause too much latency
This violates Defense in Depth — the layers are homogeneous, so a single exploit bypasses all three
Firewalls should only be used individually
questions.explanation: Defense in Depth requires heterogeneous layers. Three identical firewalls share the same vulnerabilities — if an attacker finds one bypass, all three are compromised. Better to use a WAF + network firewall + host-based firewall.

questions.question: A microservice is given database admin privileges even though it only needs to read from one table. Which principle is violated?
questions.explanation: Granting database admin privileges when only read access to one table is needed violates the Principle of Least Privilege. If the microservice is compromised, the attacker gains full database admin access.

options: Secure the Weakest Link — all technical controls were bypassed by targeting the human
Simplicity — there were too many mechanisms
questions.explanation: Despite strong technical controls, the human (admin) was the weakest link. Social engineering bypassed ALL technical defenses by targeting the most vulnerable element. This perfectly illustrates 'Secure the Weakest Link.'

questions.question: Defense in Depth is most effective when all layers use the same type of mechanism.
questions.explanation: Defense in Depth requires heterogeneous (different types of) layers. Homogeneous layers share the same vulnerabilities, so one exploit can bypass all of them.

questions.question: Incident analysis: A file upload scanner validates a path, then later opens it by filename after a user-controlled rename. Which vulnerability class is most likely?

questions.question: Incident response design: A file upload scanner validates a path, then later opens it by filename after a user-controlled rename. Provide prevent/detect/contain/recover actions.
questions.answer: Prevent: use atomic open patterns (O_NOFOLLOW, file descriptor-based checks) and avoid name-based re-resolution. Detect: alert on symlink/path anomalies and unexpected ownership changes. Contain: isolate impacted process/account and block risky file operations. Recover: restore trusted files, rotate credentials, and add regression tests for race paths.

questions.question: Which category of trust assumption is violated by buffer overflow and SQL injection attacks?
options: Software trusts users to behave ethically
Software trusts attackers have limited resources
questions.explanation: Buffer overflows and SQL injection exploit the assumption that user input is benign. These attacks send malicious input that the software processes without proper validation.

questions.question: What is a 'confused deputy' attack?
options: An attack where the user is confused about security settings
An attack where a privileged program is tricked into misusing its own authority on behalf of an attacker
An attack targeting confused error handling
An attack using multiple conflicting identities
questions.explanation: A confused deputy attack tricks a program with elevated privileges into performing unauthorized actions. The attacker leverages the program's legitimate authority rather than exploiting a direct vulnerability.

questions.question: In the Java webserver example from class, which of the following was NOT one of the four identified vulnerabilities?
questions.explanation: The four vulnerabilities in the Java webserver were: malformed HTTP requests, path traversal, large file requests, and (depending on context) improper error handling. SQL injection was not applicable since it wasn't a database-backed application.

questions.question: A user with no malicious intent accidentally causes damage because the application didn't restrict their actions. This exploits which trust assumption?
options: Software trusts users to behave appropriately
Software trusts attackers have limited resources
questions.explanation: This exploits the trust that users will behave appropriately. Even well-meaning users can cause harm if the system doesn't enforce proper restrictions on user actions.

questions.question: A web application compiles uploaded C code on its server using the system compiler. An attacker uploads a program that reads /etc/shadow. The compiler runs with elevated privileges and the compiled program inherits those privileges. What type of attack is this?
options: Confused deputy — the compiler is the 'deputy' being tricked
questions.explanation: This is a confused deputy attack. The compiler (the 'deputy') has elevated privileges for legitimate purposes, but the attacker tricks it into compiling and running malicious code that exploits those privileges.

questions.question: What is the fundamental tradeoff between security and usability?
options: Increasing security typically makes systems harder to use, and vice versa
Usability has no relationship to security
More usable systems are always more secure
questions.explanation: Security and usability are often in tension. Stronger security measures (complex passwords, MFA, access controls) typically add friction for users. Finding the right balance is a key challenge.

questions.question: Which of the following are costs of security mechanisms mentioned in the course?
options: Runtime performance, memory usage, energy consumption, and labor costs
questions.explanation: Security has multiple costs: runtime overhead (slower execution), memory usage, energy consumption, and human labor for implementation and maintenance. These must be weighed against the security benefits.

questions.question: Social engineering attacks exploit the trust assumption that users will behave appropriately.
questions.explanation: Social engineering targets users directly — tricking them into revealing information, clicking links, or taking harmful actions. It exploits the system's trust that users will follow proper procedures.

questions.question: DDoS attacks exploit the trust assumption that attackers have limited computational resources.
questions.explanation: DDoS attacks overwhelm systems with traffic from many sources, exploiting the assumption that no single attacker can generate enough requests to overwhelm the service.

questions.question: Describe the four categories of trust assumptions that software makes, and give one attack example for each.
questions.answer: 1. Trusts user input (buffer overflow, SQL injection, XSS). 2. Trusts users to behave (insider attacks, social engineering). 3. Trusts attackers have limited resources (DoS/DDoS). 4. Trusts its environment (hardware attacks, OS-level exploits, supply chain attacks).

questions.question: A company builds a secure file sharing service. They implement perfect encryption but the 'Share' button emails files to anyone without checking the recipient's authorization. Which principle and which trust assumption are most relevant?
options: Principle of Least Privilege; trusts input
Secure the Weakest Link; trusts users to behave
questions.explanation: The encryption is strong but the sharing mechanism is wide open — a classic weakest link problem. The system trusts that users will only share with authorized recipients rather than enforcing it.

questions.question: An attacker uses a path traversal attack to access '../../etc/passwd' on a web server. Which trust assumption is exploited, and which design principle would prevent it?
options: Trusts user input; Sanitize All Inputs
Trusts user behavior; Secure the Weakest Link
questions.explanation: Path traversal sends malicious input (the '../' sequences) that the server processes as a file path. This exploits trust in user input. Sanitizing inputs (validating and normalizing file paths) would prevent it.

questions.question: In the Java webserver example, explain how a path traversal attack works and why it's dangerous.
questions.answer: A path traversal attack uses relative path sequences like '../' in a URL to navigate outside the webserver's intended root directory (e.g., requesting '../../etc/passwd'). If the server doesn't validate or sanitize the requested path, the attacker can read any file the server process has access to, potentially exposing passwords, configuration files, or source code.

questions.question: Modern ransomware differs from traditional ransomware because:
options: It also exfiltrates data, meaning paying the ransom or restoring backups doesn't recover confidentiality
questions.explanation: Modern ransomware combines encryption with data exfiltration. Even if victims restore from backups (recovering availability/integrity), the stolen data means confidentiality is permanently compromised.

questions.question: A cloud service's API accepts JSON input. An attacker sends: {"role": "admin", "action": "delete_all_users"}. The API trusts the 'role' field from the request without server-side verification. Which trust assumption is exploited?
options: Trusts user input — the role field should be determined server-side, not from user-supplied data
questions.explanation: The API trusts user input (the role field) without validation. The role should be determined from the authenticated session server-side, never from client-supplied data. This is an input validation failure.

questions.question: Which type of attack specifically targets the trust assumption that attackers have limited computational resources?
questions.explanation: DDoS attacks overwhelm systems with massive traffic from many sources, directly exploiting the assumption that no attacker can marshal enough resources to overwhelm the system.

questions.question: Cross-site scripting (XSS) exploits which trust assumption?
options: Software trusts attackers have limited resources
questions.explanation: XSS attacks inject malicious scripts through user input (form fields, URLs, etc.) that the application then renders without proper sanitization, exploiting trust in user input.

questions.question: A compiler service on a shared server compiles user-submitted code with elevated privileges. An attacker submits code that includes #include "/etc/shadow". The compiler reads the shadow file as part of compilation. What type of attack is this?
options: Confused deputy attack — the compiler uses its privileges to read a file the attacker shouldn't have access to
questions.explanation: The compiler (deputy) has legitimate privileges to read files for compilation. The attacker tricks it into using those privileges to access /etc/shadow — a confused deputy attack. The compiler doesn't distinguish between legitimate includes and malicious ones.

questions.question: A confused deputy attack requires the attacker to have elevated privileges.
questions.explanation: The whole point of a confused deputy attack is that the ATTACKER doesn't need elevated privileges — they trick a program that ALREADY has elevated privileges into misusing its own authority.

questions.question: SQL injection succeeds in production. Which trust assumption failed first?
questions.explanation: SQL injection most directly exploits 'SW trusts user input'.

questions.question: Postmortem for SQL injection: what is the most targeted fix?

questions.explanation: Best targeted fix: input validation, canonicalization, and output encoding.

options: input validation, canonicalization, and output encoding

questions.explanation: False. The root trust assumption 'SW trusts user input' must be corrected directly.

questions.question: What are the three factors of authentication?
options: What you know, what you have, and what you are
Public key, private key, and certificate
questions.explanation: The three authentication factors are: what you know (password, PIN), what you have (smart card, phone), and what you are (biometrics like fingerprint, face).

questions.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
options: Authentication verifies identity; authorization determines what actions are permitted
Authentication grants permissions; authorization verifies identity
Authentication is for networks; authorization is for files
questions.explanation: Authentication establishes WHO you are (identity). Authorization determines WHAT you can do (permissions). Authentication must happen before authorization.

questions.question: In an Access Control List (ACL), permissions are attached to:
questions.explanation: ACLs attach permissions to objects — each resource has a list specifying which subjects can access it and how. This is object-centric access control.

questions.question: In a Capability List, permissions are attached to:
questions.explanation: Capability lists attach permissions to subjects — each user has a list of what resources they can access. This is subject-centric access control.

questions.question: What is Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)?
options: Users are assigned to roles, and roles have permissions — users inherit the permissions of their roles
Each user defines their own permissions
Permissions are based on time of day
questions.explanation: In RBAC, users are assigned to roles (e.g., 'admin', 'editor', 'viewer'), and permissions are assigned to roles. Users inherit the permissions of all their assigned roles. This simplifies large-scale permission management.

questions.question: In Mandatory Access Control (MAC), who determines access permissions?
options: Individual users can grant/revoke their own permissions
The system/organization determines access based on classification levels — users cannot override
Both users and the system equally
questions.explanation: In MAC, the system enforces access control based on classification levels (e.g., Top Secret, Secret, Unclassified). Users cannot override or modify these permissions. It's used in military/government contexts.

questions.question: In Discretionary Access Control (DAC), who determines access permissions?
options: Resource owners can grant/revoke access to their own resources
questions.explanation: In DAC, resource owners control access. For example, in Linux, a file owner can use chmod to grant or revoke read/write/execute permissions to others. This is the model used by most consumer operating systems.

questions.question: The Bell-LaPadula model enforces which CIA property?
questions.explanation: Bell-LaPadula enforces confidentiality through two rules: No Read Up (can't read higher classification) and No Write Down (can't write to lower classification). This prevents information from flowing downward.

questions.question: The Biba model enforces which CIA property?
questions.explanation: Biba enforces integrity through two rules: No Write Up (can't modify higher classification) and No Read Down (can't read lower classification). This prevents untrusted data from corrupting trusted data.

questions.question: In Bell-LaPadula, a user with 'Secret' clearance wants to read a 'Top Secret' document. Is this allowed?
options: Yes — they can read any document at their level or above
No — 'No Read Up' prevents reading above your clearance level
Yes — but only if they have a need-to-know
No — but only in MAC systems
questions.explanation: Bell-LaPadula's 'No Read Up' rule prevents a Secret-level user from reading Top Secret documents. Information can only flow upward (or laterally), never downward.

questions.question: In Bell-LaPadula, a user with 'Top Secret' clearance wants to write data to a 'Secret' document. Is this allowed?
options: No — 'No Write Down' prevents writing to lower classification levels
Yes — writing down is always allowed
It depends on the specific implementation
questions.explanation: Bell-LaPadula's 'No Write Down' rule prevents Top Secret users from writing to Secret documents. This prevents high-classified information from leaking to lower classification levels.

questions.question: In Biba, a user at 'High' integrity wants to read data from a 'Low' integrity source. Is this allowed?
options: Yes — they can read anything at or below their level
No — 'No Read Down' prevents reading lower integrity data
Yes — reading down is always safe
Only if the data is encrypted
questions.explanation: Biba's 'No Read Down' prevents reading lower integrity data. If a trusted user reads untrusted data and acts on it, the integrity of their decisions could be compromised.

questions.question: If a system enforces BOTH Bell-LaPadula AND Biba simultaneously, what access is permitted?
options: Full read and write at all levels
Read up and write down only
Read down and write up only
Users can only read and write at their own classification level
questions.explanation: Bell-LaPadula: No Read Up, No Write Down. Biba: No Read Down, No Write Up. Combined: no reading or writing up OR down. Users can only access their own level — very restrictive.

questions.question: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) uses two or more factors from the same category.
questions.explanation: True MFA requires factors from DIFFERENT categories (e.g., something you know + something you have). Two passwords (both 'something you know') would NOT be MFA.

questions.question: Linux file permissions (chmod) are an example of Discretionary Access Control.
questions.explanation: Linux uses DAC — file owners can use chmod to discretionally grant or revoke permissions on their own files.

questions.question: In MAC, a regular user can grant another user access to a classified document they own.
questions.explanation: In MAC, the system determines access based on classification levels. Users cannot override these permissions, unlike in DAC where owners can share their files.

questions.question: Compare ACLs and Capability Lists. In what scenario would you prefer each?
questions.answer: ACLs (attached to objects) are better when you need to answer 'who can access this resource?' — useful for auditing resource access. Capability lists (attached to subjects) are better for answering 'what can this user do?' — useful for managing user permissions. ACLs are common in file systems; capabilities are common in distributed systems and mobile app permissions.

questions.question: Explain why combining Bell-LaPadula and Biba results in extremely restrictive access rules.
questions.answer: Bell-LaPadula (confidentiality): No Read Up + No Write Down. Biba (integrity): No Read Down + No Write Up. Combined, you cannot read up or down, and cannot write up or down. The only remaining access is reading and writing at exactly your own classification level, which is very restrictive and often impractical.

questions.question: A fingerprint scanner is an example of which authentication factor?
questions.explanation: Biometrics (fingerprint, face, iris) are 'something you are' — physical characteristics unique to the individual.

questions.question: An Access Control Matrix (ACM) is:
options: A 2D matrix mapping subjects to objects with permissions at each intersection
questions.explanation: An ACM is a 2D matrix where rows represent subjects, columns represent objects, and each cell contains the permissions that subject has on that object. ACLs and capability lists are just different views of this matrix.

questions.question: A military analyst at 'Secret' clearance writes a report and saves it to a 'Top Secret' folder. Under Bell-LaPadula, is this allowed? What about under Biba?
options: Bell-LaPadula: Yes (write up is fine). Biba: No (no write up)
questions.explanation: Bell-LaPadula allows writing up (only forbids writing DOWN to protect confidentiality). Biba forbids writing up (to protect integrity — lower integrity shouldn't modify higher integrity data).

questions.question: MLS (Multi-Level Security) with levels Top Secret > Secret > Public is most closely associated with:
questions.explanation: Multi-Level Security with hierarchical classification levels is a hallmark of Mandatory Access Control (MAC), where the system enforces access based on these levels.

questions.question: A company assigns developers to the 'Dev' role and auditors to the 'Audit' role. A developer who also serves as an internal auditor needs both sets of permissions. Under RBAC, how is this handled?
options: Create a third 'Dev-Audit' role combining permissions
Assign the user to BOTH the Dev and Audit roles — users can hold multiple roles
Grant individual exceptions outside the RBAC system
questions.explanation: In RBAC, users can be assigned to multiple roles and inherit the combined permissions. This is a core feature of RBAC that provides flexibility while maintaining structured access control.

questions.question: A user authenticates with both a password and a fingerprint scan. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: This combines 'something you know' (password) with 'something you are' (fingerprint) — two different factors, making it two-factor authentication (2FA).

questions.question: In a system using Bell-LaPadula, a 'Top Secret' user creates a document. At what classification level is the document?
options: At the user's level (Top Secret) or higher
questions.explanation: Under Bell-LaPadula's 'No Write Down' rule, a Top Secret user can only write at their own level or above. The document would be classified at Top Secret level (or higher if such levels exist).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the Bell-LaPadula rules?
options: No Read Down, No Write Up
No Read Up, No Write Down
No Read Up, No Write Up
No Read Down, No Write Down
questions.explanation: Bell-LaPadula (confidentiality): No Read Up (can't read above your level) and No Write Down (can't write below your level). This prevents information from flowing from high to low classification.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the Biba rules?
options: No Read Up, No Write Down
No Read Down, No Write Up
No Read Up, No Write Up
No Read Down, No Write Down
questions.explanation: Biba (integrity): No Read Down (can't read below your level) and No Write Up (can't write above your level). This prevents untrusted data from corrupting trusted data.

questions.question: In DAC, the operating system alone determines access permissions without user input.
questions.explanation: In DAC (Discretionary Access Control), resource OWNERS can grant and revoke access to their own resources. That's what makes it 'discretionary.' MAC is where the system alone determines access.

questions.answer: Use RBAC for normal duties, MAC-style labeling for highly sensitive records, and tightly controlled break-glass workflows with time limits, mandatory justification, and immutable audit logs. Enforce complete mediation on every read/write action.

questions.question: How should a company migrate from ad-hoc ACL sprawl to safer RBAC without disrupting production?
questions.answer: Inventory current permissions, group by job function, design minimal roles, map users incrementally, simulate effective permissions, audit for privilege creep, and remove direct grants in phases with rollback and recertification checkpoints.

Move logs to a different color theme
questions.explanation: Per-request enforcement closes stale-decision windows and aligns behavior with complete mediation.
